title: Free speech and social change: a public reason approach
author: Bonotti, Nwokora

opening_move.description: The paper opens by diagnosing a legitimacy crisis for free speech in contemporary liberal democracies: social changes (individualism, migration, post-truth, cognitive biases, technology) have altered the empirical context in which the three classical arguments for free speech were developed, requiring their re-evaluation.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: The protection of free speech must be re-legitimized through a 'public reason test' that accounts for social change; without this, free speech protections lose their democratic justification

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper conducts a systematic re-evaluation of the three main arguments for free speech through the lens of Rawlsian public reason, arguing that only the democracy argument survives the test of contemporary social change.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: qualitative analysis of social change processes (individualism, migration, post-truth, cognitive bias research, technological change)
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper uses empirical descriptions of social change processes to assess the viability of normative arguments — empirical material is supportive but not constitutive of the normative conclusion.

normative_stake.domain: democratic theory / free speech / constitutional law

confidence_notes: Full-length article (21 pages). The 'normative_with_qualitative_empirical' classification reflects the paper's use of social science descriptions of five change processes as inputs to the normative argument; the argument itself is normative-analytical.
